---
title: "Labor OS and Typed Jobs"
chapter_id: "labor-os-and-typed-jobs"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "talos"
  - "viea"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "talos_md"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact source-note mappings for the core claim, and four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `talos_md` and `moecot` remain connector/source-note mapped."
  - "Protocol schema/fixture validation and narrow Lean lifecycle/approval predicates are implemented; no job scheduler, permission-enforcement harness, adapter runner, or replay harness has been run for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `labor-os-and-typed-jobs` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `talos`, `viea`, `genesiscode`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `talos_md`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `talos`; supporting: `viea`, `genesiscode`, `software_magic_grimoire`; variant: `talos_md`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | passage-reviewed local raw cache: `talos`, `viea`, `genesiscode`, `software_magic_grimoire`; connector/source-note mapped: `talos_md`, `moecot` |
| Test state | Typed job fixture validation implemented; finite lifecycle and approval predicates implemented in `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs`; `python3 scripts/validate_plan_execution_contracts.py` checks synthetic active-job receipt and approval-bypass cases; scheduler, permission, adapter, replay, and deployed approval-service tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats typed jobs as the execution contract for work, not as proof that a labor OS is implemented. The repo validates a typed-job fixture and now proves two finite Lean predicates over lifecycle and approval records, but it has not run a job scheduler, permission harness, adapter runner, approval service, or replay system for this chapter.

Placed after tribunal review, this chapter is the first execution translation point. Reviewed claims, accepted plans, and unresolved required actions cannot remain conversational objects; they must become bounded work packets whose state, authority, approvals, and failure behavior can be inspected.

The typed job is where the stack stops planning and starts owing receipts. A plan node describes intended work; a job packet records authorized work with lifecycle, permission, approval, artifact, and replay obligations attached.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

After planning, verification, and review, the stack has to turn accepted work into bounded execution. A Labor OS is the layer where tasks become typed jobs with lifecycle, parentage, inputs, outputs, approvals, isolation, logs, failure behavior, and completion criteria.

This is where "agent" becomes less useful as a word. The architecture does not need a free-floating actor; it needs jobs that say what they are allowed to do, which gate can stop them, what evidence they must return, and how their work can be audited or replayed later. A job is small enough to govern and durable enough to inspect.

Typed work turns execution from a personality problem into a recordkeeping and authority problem.

That shift is what makes large-scale AI labor auditable rather than mysterious. The work item becomes something a team can inspect, replay, deny, or improve.

Execution becomes safer when the smallest useful unit is a record rather than a persona, because typed work makes delegation reviewable before personality enters the story. Records precede delegation so execution remains bounded after handoff begins downstream.
:::

## Problem

Intelligence must become typed work with lifecycle, permissions, artifacts, logs, and approvals.

The previous chapters decide what should be believed and reviewed. Execution begins when accepted plans become work. Talos supplies the right frame: an AI labor operating system turns plans into typed jobs with contract locks, permissions, isolated workspaces, adjudication, delivery, feedback, audit, and replay.

A job is the unit that keeps agency bounded. It carries a contract, lifecycle state, inputs, outputs, permissions, approval state, runtime adapter, failure behavior, audit events, and replay status.

This makes jobs the execution-side partner of command contracts. Commands expose the semantic fields; plans decompose those fields; jobs bind each dispatchable node to an execution state that can be approved, denied, audited, retried, or retired.

The job boundary is also where the stack distinguishes work from evidence. A job can run and produce something useful without proving that the work was authorized, verified, replayable, or safe to reuse. The labor OS should make that difference explicit instead of collapsing it into a single "done" status.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Chat outputs do not provide deterministic job lifecycle, tool isolation, auditability, or repeatable cognitive manufacturing.

Chat is not a factory. It does not lock a contract, isolate tools, enforce permission boundaries, require approvals, preserve logs, or tell a later verifier how to replay what happened. Without jobs, the system has vibes and side effects instead of controlled work.

AutoGen (`ext_autogen_2023`) gives an external multi-agent workflow baseline, and SWE-bench (`ext_swe_bench_2023`) gives an external software-task benchmark baseline with repository-scale context and patch evaluation. Labor OS uses those comparison points to ask for typed job lifecycles, artifact receipts, replay, and approval boundaries around agentic work. The baseline references do not claim that Talos jobs reproduce AutoGen workflows or solve SWE-bench tasks.

GenesisCode reinforces the distinction: proposals and effects are different things. Effects require capability boundaries and replay logs. Software Magic adds that workflows need entry rules, exit rules, guards, loops, and recovery paths.

Agent swarms often fail at this boundary. They start many plausible subtasks but lose the lifecycle state, permission scope, parent contract, or artifact trail that would let a reviewer know which work was authorized and which work merely happened. A Labor OS should make unauthorized work hard to start and unlogged work hard to hide.

The same failure can happen in a single-agent workflow. A model may create files, messages, patches, summaries, or decisions that look complete but lack approval state, effect receipts, or replay metadata. The unit of governance is therefore not the agent; it is the typed job record.

## Core Claim

[labor-os-and-typed-jobs.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] The execution layer should convert plans into typed jobs managed by a governed labor operating system.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Talos supplies the labor-OS frame, contract-lock discipline, job pipeline, evidence-bound artifact, audit, replay, and explicit non-claim boundaries. VIEA supplies the intent-to-artifact-to-runtime trace, permission envelopes, approval roles, side-effect classes, runtime-adapter maturity levels, and feedback loop. GenesisCode supplies the proposal/effect separation, host-provided capability boundary, deterministic effect logs, replay semantics, obligations, provenance, and semantic patch discipline. Software Magic Grimoire supplies workflow guard vocabulary for bounded instruction artifacts, output contracts, verification, failure behavior, and gated spell stacks. The Markdown Talos release and MoECOT remain connector/source-note mapped context, not passage-reviewed evidence.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this labor-OS claim to all assigned source notes, and four local raw-cache mappings now carry passage-reviewed references. The mappings support typed jobs, lifecycle gates, permissions, evidence records, replay, and approval boundaries as execution architecture, not a working scheduler or runtime.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `talos` | Passage-reviewed: Operating System for AI Labor and control-plane framing, contract lock, MVI routing, adversarial verification, atomic decomposition, blind execution, observability, factory-to-tribunal job flow, deterministic cognitive manufacturing, job-based asynchronous execution, fixed execution pipeline, intent sanitation, claim extraction, evidence binding, proof bundles, hashed/auditable I/O, replay/reverification/evidence audit, typed claim/evidence/state/artifact records, and explicit non-claims about truth/correctness/SME replacement. | No Talos scheduler, approval service, runtime, replay log, benchmark, security result, or proof bundle has been reproduced here. |
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: generation-not-execution framing, intent to command to artifact graph to routed specialist execution to runtime target to verification gate to execution and feedback, command contracts, artifact graph, permission model, specialist/runtime adapter subsystems, least-privilege permission envelopes, approval roles, integration contracts, side-effect classes, rollback limits, irreversible side-effect gates, runtime-adapter maturity levels, and runtime-specific verification. | No deployed intent-to-job pipeline, permission service, runtime adapter, feedback loop, or verified execution trace exists here. |
| `genesiscode` | Passage-reviewed: untrusted AI-output premise, small pure kernel, capability boundary for nondeterminism, deterministic effect logs, replay semantics, obligation engine, canonical IR validators, semantic patches, AI as proposer not authority, sealed effect requests, host-provided capabilities, evidence artifacts, registry rejection until obligations pass, content-addressed packages, and accepted-patch provenance. | No local GenesisCode implementation, effect runner, replay checker, proof artifact, security audit, registry policy, or obligation run exists here. |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Passage-reviewed: bounded instruction artifacts, artifact/objective/constraint/output/truth-condition framing, eight-limb role/objective/context/constraints/procedure/output-contract/verification/failure-behavior anatomy, risk-scaled cast levels, exploration/execution separation, artifact/invariant/search/output/verification/failure workflow, prompt pathologies, spell-stack coordination, FEATURE-RAIL, loop rules, and verification-bundle shape. | Workflow vocabulary only; not empirical evidence that a labor OS performs better or safely executes work. |
| `talos_md` | Connector/source-note mapped: public-release Talos terminology around typed AI labor, deterministic manufacturing, five-phase lifecycle, control-plane ledgers, evidence, isolation, auditability, blind handles, and Digital SCIFs. | Variant/canonicalization aid only; local cache is an auth-gate placeholder, and reported benchmark or security claims remain unverified. |
| `moecot` | Connector/source-note mapped: runtime-reference context for governed orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, promotion blockers, and residual tracking. | Runtime code, logs, benchmark artifacts, and replay records have not been inspected or reproduced here. |

## Mechanism

The execution layer is where a plan stops being an intention and becomes a governed unit of work. Talos supplies the labor-OS shape: contract lock, fixed pipeline, evidence-bound artifact, audit, and replay. VIEA keeps that work inside the larger intent-to-artifact-to-runtime trace and adds permission envelopes, approval roles, side-effect classes, and runtime maturity levels. GenesisCode sharpens the boundary between proposals and effects: capabilities come from the host runner, effects produce logs, and acceptance depends on obligations and provenance. Software Magic Grimoire gives the workflow vocabulary for bounded instruction artifacts, output contracts, verification, failure behavior, gates, loops, and stop conditions.

A Typed Job is the packet that binds those ideas together. It says what kind of work this is, which contract authorized it, which inputs and tools are allowed, which artifact is expected, which approval state blocks dispatch, how failure is handled, and what audit trail must survive.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Plan node"] --> B["Typed job"]
  B --> C["Contract lock"]
  C --> D["Permission check"]
  D --> E{"Approval required?"}
  E -- "yes" --> F["Approval gate"]
  E -- "no" --> G["Dispatch"]
  F --> G
  G --> H["Runtime adapter"]
  H --> I["Artifact + audit events"]
  I --> J["Adjudication / delivery"]
  J --> K["Feedback + replay status"]
```

**Reading the typed job lifecycle:** The execution flow is a lifecycle gate around work, not a direct line from plan to tool. A typed job is locked, checked, approved when necessary, dispatched through an adapter, and returned with artifact, audit, feedback, and replay state.

The job packet binds work type, inputs, allowed tools, permissions, expected artifacts, approval gates, failure behavior, and replay expectations before dispatch. Dispatch is not a model decision alone; it is a lifecycle transition that can be rejected, quarantined, replayed, or retired. Orchestration, secure workspace setup, access control, secret isolation, adapter invocation, adjudication, delivery, feedback, and replay stay separate so a failure in one boundary does not disappear into the general idea of "the agent did it."

The lifecycle should be explicit enough to reject invalid transitions. A job can be drafted, locked, awaiting approval, dispatchable, running, adjudicating, delivered, failed, blocked, replayed, or retired. High-impact work should stop at the approval gate until the required record exists.

The execution layer should not merge all subfunctions into one agent. Orchestration, secure workspace setup, access control, adapter invocation, artifact capture, adjudication, delivery, feedback, and replay are different boundaries.

Typed jobs should also preserve parentage. A reviewer should be able to trace each job back to a command contract, plan node, approval record if required, runtime adapter, produced artifact, and residual. If that chain breaks, the job may still be useful work, but it should not count as governed execution.

### Typed job lifecycle states

Typed jobs should use explicit lifecycle states:

| State | Meaning | Evidence consequence |
|---|---|---|
| `drafted` | A job packet exists but is not contract-locked. | No dispatch. |
| `locked` | Contract, parentage, expected artifact, and failure behavior are fixed. | Eligible for permission review. |
| `awaiting_approval` | Required approval is missing or stale. | Cannot run. |
| `approved` | Required approval is present and scoped. | Eligible for dispatch if permissions pass. |
| `dispatchable` | Permissions, approvals, inputs, and adapter requirements are satisfied. | May run. |
| `running` | Runtime work is active. | Audit events required. |
| `adjudicating` | Output exists but verification, review, or acceptance is pending. | Not evidence-ready. |
| `delivered` | Output has been returned or made available. | Useful artifact; support still bounded. |
| `evidence_ready` | Required artifact capture, verification, audit, residual, and replay/non-replay fields exist. | Candidate evidence for downstream ledgers. |
| `failed_or_blocked` | The job failed, was denied, or lacks a required gate. | Residual and recovery path required. |
| `quarantined` | The job had unauthorized effects, missing provenance, or unsafe behavior. | Cannot support promotion. |
| `replayed_or_retired` | Replay completed or the job is no longer valid for reuse. | Historical trace only unless requalified. |

The distinction between `delivered` and `evidence_ready` is central. Delivery satisfies a user-facing workflow; evidence readiness satisfies the living stack.

## Interfaces

Typed work enters execution as the Typed Job.

- Planning dispatches jobs.
- Security mediates permissions.
- Artifact graph receives outputs.

Minimum fields:

- `job_id`
- `contract_id`
- `job_type`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `runtime_adapter`
- `inputs`
- `outputs`
- `permissions`
- `approval_state`
- `failure_behavior`
- `audit_events`
- `replay_status`

Planning dispatches jobs. Security mediates permissions. Runtime adapters perform effects. Artifact graphs receive outputs, logs, and replay metadata.

The job interface should carry enough state for other layers to refuse it. Security can reject missing permissions. Approval can block high-impact work. Runtime adapters can reject unsupported capabilities. Artifact graphs can mark incomplete provenance. Evidence can refuse to treat delivery as support until adjudication and replay fields exist.

The job should emit a completion receipt:

- `parent_contract_id`
- `plan_node_id`
- `approval_record`
- `permission_basis`
- `runtime_adapter_ref`
- `artifact_refs`
- `verification_refs`
- `audit_event_refs`
- `residuals`
- `replay_or_non_replay_reason`
- `delivery_state`
- `evidence_state`

This receipt is what prevents completion laundering. A job can be delivered while its evidence state remains incomplete, disputed, blocked, or quarantined.

## Invariants

- Every job has type and lifecycle state.
- Tool permissions are explicit.
- Human approval gates block high-impact jobs.
- Delivered output is not evidence-ready until verification, artifact capture, audit, residual, and replay/non-replay fields are present.
- Job parentage remains traceable to contract, plan node, approval, adapter, artifact, and residual.

The approval invariant is non-negotiable: a job requiring approval cannot execute because a planner or model predicts approval would be granted. The approval record is part of the job state.

Effect accountability keeps typed labor from becoming ambient action. Every external effect should have an adapter reference, permission basis, audit event, and replay or non-replay declaration. Otherwise the labor OS has produced side effects without a work record.

## Failure modes

- Tool overreach.
- Unlogged side effects.
- Infinite or chaotic agent swarms.
- Completion laundering, where output existence is treated as governed evidence.
- Parentage loss, where a job cannot be traced back to the command contract and dispatchable plan node that authorized it.

Tool overreach is a permission failure. Unlogged side effects are audit failures. Chaotic agent swarms are lifecycle failures: work escapes the job boundary and becomes unreviewable. Each should block dispatch or force quarantine.

Completion laundering is the typed-job failure. A job can be marked done because an output exists, even though required approval, verification, artifact capture, or replay metadata is missing. Delivery should not be the same state as evidence-ready completion.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A typed-job schema with lifecycle states and permission checks.

The labor OS starts with the expanded `typed_job` schema and fixture. It validates lifecycle, approval, failure behavior, permissions, audit events, and replay status while leaving real job execution unclaimed.

The typed-job fixture checks the job record surface. `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs` adds a bounded transition predicate for declared lifecycle moves and a bounded approval predicate showing that an approval-required job cannot be allowed to run without an approval record. Adapter permission checks, scheduler implementation, approval-service integration, and replay results remain separate artifacts.

A small lifecycle harness over fixture jobs should come before any scheduler claim: one valid transition path, one invalid transition, one missing approval, one missing permission, and one delivered-but-not-evidence-ready job. The harness would exercise the labor-OS boundary while keeping deployed scheduling unclaimed.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The labor layer reaches its mature form as an operating system for bounded cognitive work. It refuses ambient "agent" actors and admits work only as typed jobs whose parent contract, plan node, lifecycle state, permission basis, approval record, workspace, adapter route, expected artifact, failure behavior, audit trail, delivery state, and evidence state are explicit.

In a labor OS, every job can be refused, approved, dispatched, adjudicated, delivered, quarantined, replayed, or retired through declared transitions. Delivery and evidence readiness remain separate: a user-visible output may exist while verification, artifact capture, replay declaration, or residual accounting is still incomplete. That separation prevents useful output from being laundered into governed evidence.

A bounded labor OS needs:

- A job lifecycle engine that rejects invalid transitions and preserves parentage from command contract to plan node to approval to adapter to artifact.
- Permission and approval services that block high-impact work until scoped records exist.
- Workspace and isolation controls for source access, secret handles, tool calls, runtime effects, and audit capture.
- Completion receipts that separate request, approval, execution, artifact capture, verification, delivery, residuals, and replay/non-replay state.
- Operator surfaces that show drafted, blocked, awaiting-approval, running, adjudicating, delivered, evidence-ready, quarantined, replayed, and retired work.

This accountable execution layer is still an architectural target. Leave typed-job support at `argument` until lifecycle harnesses, permission checks, approval services, adapter runners, completion receipts, and replayable job traces show that intelligence produces work packets with receipts instead of unmanaged activity.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Typed job fixture validation | Check that the typed-job fixture matches the public schema and declares contract, type, lifecycle state, adapter, inputs, outputs, permissions, approval state, failure behavior, audit events, and replay status. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Typed job lifecycle predicate | Check that a transition record marked valid uses the declared finite lifecycle relation. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs`; checked by Lean build |
| Plan-execution typed-job dispatch harness | Check that active typed jobs match the command contract, appear in dispatch receipts, and do not bypass required approval. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_plan_execution_contracts.py`; synthetic records only |
| Tool permission enforcement test | Check that declared permissions cover requested adapter capabilities. | planned; not run |
| Human approval gate predicate | Check that an approval-required job cannot be allowed to run before approval is recorded. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs`; checked by Lean build |
| Delivery versus evidence-ready test | Check that delivered jobs without verification, artifact capture, audit, residual, and replay/non-replay fields cannot support promotion. | planned; not run |
| Job parentage trace test | Check that each job traces to parent contract, plan node, approval if needed, adapter, artifact, and residual records. | planned; not run |

Fixture-shape validation and two bounded Lean predicates are implemented. The remaining tests require a lifecycle checker, permission harness, approval service, adapter runner, or job runner that can reject invalid transitions, missing approvals, and unauthorized effects in an executable environment.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:jobs.lifecycle.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs` | A job transitions only through valid lifecycle states. | implemented |
| `lean:jobs.lifecycle.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.TypedJobs` | A job requiring approval cannot execute before approval is recorded. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates. They prove the bounded lifecycle and approval claims stated above; they do not prove the existence or correctness of a full labor-OS runtime.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `talos_md` | Talos_Protocol_v1.0.md | labor_execution_os | Markdown/public release version. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The labor-OS sources support typed execution discipline across lifecycle, permissions, approvals, artifacts, and replay. Four local raw-cache sources now have passage-reviewed mappings, while `talos_md` and `moecot` remain connector/source-note mapped. This does not claim a Talos, MoECOT, GenesisCode, or VIEA labor runtime has been executed in this repo.

## Summary

Labor OS and Typed Jobs turns plans into controlled work. It gives each executable unit a type, contract, lifecycle, permissions, approval state, adapter, artifacts, failure behavior, audit events, and replay status. The sources justify treating execution as typed work, but this repo still lacks the scheduler, permission service, approval service, adapter runner, and replay system that would turn the boundary into runtime evidence.

Typed jobs do not reduce autonomy; they make autonomy inspectable, approvable, replayable, repairable, and improvable. The job's output then enters the artifact graph, where work becomes durable memory instead of a disappearing side effect.

The handoff to artifacts should be mandatory. A job that leaves no artifact, audit event, residual, or replay declaration has not become part of the living stack's memory, even if it produced a useful answer.

## Handoff

Typed jobs give execution a bounded work unit, but the stack still needs a durable record of what each job produced, read, changed, and failed to prove. Artifact Graphs, Audit Logs, and Replay supplies that continuity layer. It makes outputs, sources, context transactions, tools, audit events, replay limits, and residuals part of the system's memory rather than leftovers around the work.
